title: Chapter 30: The Visit
content: The smell of butter is everywhere.

It's in the air, it's in the walls, it's in my hair. I've watched Julian Blake do many impossible things—survive a war, run into a collapsing building, love someone through the end of the world—but I have never seen him bake. And now, watching him pull a second tray of scones from the oven while muttering what I'm fairly sure are Gaelic curses, I understand that this is the true disaster. Not the multiverse. Not the Fork app. This. Julian Blake, baking.
"Is that—" I start.
"Don't."
"I was going to say they look good."

"They look acceptable." He sets the tray on the counter with the precision of a man defusing a bomb. The scones are golden, slightly lopsided, freckled with raisins. They smell like warm butter and brown sugar and someone trying very, very hard. "They need to look better than acceptable. She'll inspect them."
"Your mother inspects scones?"
"She inspects everything." He wipes flour from his hands onto a tea towel, leaves a white ghost-print on his jaw, doesn't notice. "She'll have opinions about the café. About the cats. About you."
"About me?"
"Avery." He turns, and there's something raw in his expression—hope and terror braided together. "She hasn't seen me in three years. The last time I was— I wasn't—"
I cross the kitchen in three steps and take his flour-dusted hands. "You were still you. She'll see that."
His throat works. He nods. Then he looks down at our intertwined hands and says, "I have flour in places flour shouldn't be."
"I know. It's in your eyebrow. And your ear. How did you get it in your ear?"
"Baking is a contact sport."

I brush the flour from his eyebrow, his jaw, the ridiculous place near his temple. He closes his eyes and leans into my hand for just a moment, and I feel the tremor in him—not fear, exactly, but the vibration of someone about to step onto a stage after years in the wings.

It's been four months since the Choice. Four months since I collapsed every probability wave and picked this life, this world, this man. Clonakilty in early summer has turned impossibly green, the kind of green that makes you understand why people write poems about Ireland. The sea is silver-blue outside the café window, and the cats are draped over every surface like furry decorative throws, and Julian has baked scones, and his mother is coming.

The café is immaculate. I've scrubbed the espresso machine twice. I've rearranged the bookshelf by color and then by author and then by color again because I was nervous and needed something to do with my hands. The wildflowers on the tables are fresh from the garden. The windows are open and the salt air mingles with the butter smell, and I think: this is a life. This is what a life looks like when you stop splitting it into pieces.
A car comes down the lane.
Julian goes still. His hand tightens around the tea towel. I watch him breathe once, twice, three times—military breathing, the kind that gets you through things—and then he walks to the door.
Margaret Blake steps out of a small green car into the afternoon light.

She's smaller than I expected. Silver hair cut practical and short, a cardigan the color of autumn leaves, sensible shoes. But her spine is straight as a cathedral pillar, and when she looks up at the café—her son's café, this improbable thing he built from ruin—her face does something complicated. Pride and grief and something else I can't name.
Then she sees Julian.

The silence stretches. Three years of silence, of phone calls declined and letters unanswered, of a mother watching her son disappear into himself from across a country. Three years compressed into this moment on a doorstep in West Cork.
Margaret Blake opens her arms.
Julian walks into them.

He's a head taller than her, broad-shouldered, a man who has carried other men out of burning buildings, and he folds into his mother's embrace like he's seven years old. She holds the back of his head with one hand, the way I do, the way you hold someone you're afraid will vanish. I stand inside the café doorway and let them have this.

Eventually, Margaret pulls back. She cups his face in both hands and examines him—his eyes, his jaw, the scar above his brow—with a thoroughness that makes his flour inspection look casual. "You've colour in your cheeks," she says. Her voice is Dublin, working-class, unadorned. "That's new."
"I've been eating properly."
"Liar. She's been making you eat properly." Margaret's eyes cut past him to me.

And here it is. The evaluating look. The one every partner dreads, the one that decides whether you're worthy or wanting. Margaret Blake looks at me the way generals assess terrain—strategic, thorough, missing nothing. She clocks my posture, my clothes, my position beside her son. She sees the café behind me, the cats, the sea view. She sees everything.
I hold her gaze.

I've faced Wall Street boardrooms full of men who wanted me to fail. I've sat across from Parisian socialites who evaluated me like a stock ticker. I've watched the architecture of spacetime fold in on itself. I can handle one Irish mother.
"He says you saved his life," Margaret says. Not a question. A statement, delivered flat, with room for my response to change her mind.
"He saved his own life," I say. "I just helped with the bandages."

Margaret's mouth twitches. It's the ghost of a smile—the kind that starts before the face gives permission. "You'll do," she says. Then she walks past me into the café like she owns it, which, I suspect, is how Margaret Blake enters every room.

The afternoon unfolds like a slow exhalation. Julian serves his scones—still warm, split open, dripping with butter and blackberry jam I made last week—and Margaret takes a bite and closes her eyes and says nothing, which is apparently the highest compliment in the Blake family lexicon. Tea steeps in the pot, dark and strong. The cats perform their diplomatic duties: Bran pads over to Margaret and lies across her feet like he's known her forever; Nebel jumps onto the windowsill and judges from a distance; Luna investigates Margaret's handbag and emerges with a tissue, triumphant.
"She named the grey one after a Celtic god," Julian says, pouring more tea.
"I named him after a dog in a story," I say. "He named himself after the god."
"The cat chose," Julian confirms, as if this is a normal sentence.
Margaret listens. And then, as the afternoon tilts toward gold, she tells stories.

Young Julian, age seven, bringing home every stray animal in Dublin. A dog with three legs. A cat with one eye. A hedgehog. An actual hedgehog, Margaret emphasizes, staring at her son like she still can't believe it. "He kept it in a shoebox. Fed it slugs. Slugs, Julian."
"They were garden slugs, they were clean—"
"There is no such thing as a clean slug."

Young Julian, age fifteen, standing up in religion class to argue with Father Donnelly about social justice and the Church's responsibility to the poor. "Father Donnelly was a good man," Margaret says, "but he had no idea what to do with a boy who'd actually read the doctrine." Julian shrugs, embarrassed. "He cried when I said the Church should give away its gold." "He cried because you were right and he knew it."

Young Julian, age twenty, writing letters home from overseas. Not about the war—never about the war—but about the children. The kids in the village who followed the soldiers around. The little girl who learned to count in English from him. The boy who wanted to be a doctor. "He wrote about them like they were his own," Margaret says quietly. "Every week. The same children. I kept all the letters."
Julian is looking at his tea. His jaw is tight. I can see the war behind his eyes, the things he doesn't write letters about.
"Together we have nine," Margaret says, and her voice is gentle now, "but the letters stopped when he came home."
A silence. The sea fills it.
"I'm sorry, Mam," Julian says.
"Don't be sorry. Be here." She reaches across the table and takes his hand. Holds it. The afternoon light catches the silver in her hair and turns it to wire and silk.

I see him through her eyes. Not the Julian I met—broken, guarded, playing his music in an empty pub—but the boy who couldn't pass a stray, the teenager who argued with priests, the soldier who wrote about children. A lifetime of choosing others, long before I ever arrived. Long before the Fork app, before the multiverse, before any of it. The core of him, the thing that has always been true: he sees the ones who need help, and he goes to them.
No wonder he saw me.

Margaret leaves as the evening settles in, the sky going peach and lilac over the water. She hugs Julian at the door—long, fierce, the kind of hug that says I'm not losing you again. Then she turns to me.

The hug catches me off guard. It's small and strong and smells like tea and lavender soap and something underneath that's just—mother. My own mother hasn't hugged me like this in years. Maybe ever. Margaret Blake holds me with the certainty of someone who's made a decision, and the decision is that I belong here.
"You take care of him," she says against my ear. Not a demand. A trust.
"He takes care of me, too."

She pulls back and looks at me. That evaluating look again, but softer now. Warmer. "That's the right answer." She squeezes my arms once, lets go, and walks to her car. Small, silver-haired, implacable. Dublin iron.
Julian stands in the doorway, watching. The red taillights disappear down the lane, round the bend, and then there's only the sound of the sea and the early evening wind in the wildflowers.
"I forgot what it felt like," he says. His voice is rough, scraped. "Having someone who knew you before everything went wrong."
I step beside him and take his hand. His fingers are cold. I lace mine through them and hold on.
"She still knows you," I say. "The you underneath all of it."
He turns his head and looks at me. Those warm brown eyes—his mother's eyes—dark with something I can't quite name. Gratitude, maybe. Or recognition.
"So do you," he says.

The night comes in soft and slow. After closing, the café belongs to us—the way it always does, the way we've let it become ours. The chairs are up on the tables. The espresso machine is quiet. The sea is a murmur through the half-open door. Bran and Luna are curled together on the piano bench. Nebel has claimed the spot by the heater like the small tyrant he is.
Julian disappears into the back and comes out holding his guitar.

He hasn't played since— I don't know. Before. Not like this, anyway. Not just for the sake of making sound. He sits in the window seat, the last light of evening silver behind him, and rests the guitar on his thigh. His fingers move over the strings without pressing, just touching, reacquainting.
Then he plucks a note. Just one. It rings out clear and true and hangs in the air like a held breath.

He tries another. And another. A pattern emerges—not a song, not yet, but the shape of one, the bones of one. His brow furrows. His fingers find a chord that surprises him. He plays it again, holding it longer, letting it breathe.

The melody comes slowly. It's not the music he used to play in the pub—those practiced, performed songs, delivered like armor. This is something else. Raw and searching, like a hand reaching through the dark. It rises and falls, stumbles, finds its footing, starts again. There's no audience. There's no stage. There's just a man and a guitar and a café at the edge of the sea, and the music he's making is the sound of someone coming back to himself.
I sit on the counter and pull my knees up and listen.

Bran lifts his head. Luna opens one eye. Nebel, the unflappable, actually stands up and walks to Julian's feet and sits down, which I've never seen him do. The melody fills the café the way candlelight fills a room—softly, from the edges inward, until everything is warmed by it. The strings hum. Julian's hands are sure now, muscle memory and something deeper, something that doesn't need memory because it was never really gone.
He plays, and I watch his face in the half-dark, and I think: this is what healing looks like. Not the absence of damage. The presence of something growing over it.
The melody trails off, unresolved, the way real life does. Julian sits with his hands still on the strings, looking out at the dark water.
"Play more," I say.
"Later." He sets the guitar aside. "That's enough for now."

I check my phone. Habit. The reflex of a woman who once lived by notifications that split reality into branching paths. Still no Fork app. The icon hasn't reappeared. The silence where it used to be is no longer an absence but a presence—a space that's been filled by other things. By mornings. By espresso steam and cat fur and the sound of Julian laughing at his own terrible jokes. By this.

But something catches my eye. A notification I've never seen before. Not from the Fork app—from a different app entirely. A meditation app I downloaded months ago and never used. I don't remember downloading it, honestly. It must have been one of those late-night, anxiety-driven App Store scrolls, the digital equivalent of buying a gym membership at 2 AM.
The notification says: **You've been present for 120 days. Consistency matters. Keep going.**
I laugh out loud. Not a polite laugh—the real kind, the kind that startles the cats and makes Julian look up with concern.
"What's funny?" He's already smiling, the way he does when I laugh for no reason he can identify, like my joy is a mystery he's happy to live inside.
I show him the notification.

He reads it. And then—then he smiles. Not a grin. Not a smirk. Not the careful, guarded half-smile he wears like a shield. The real one. The rare one that starts in his eyes and spreads until his whole face is transformed, warm and open and unafraid.
"Not bad," he says.
Not bad at all.

One hundred and twenty days of being present. One hundred and twenty days of choosing one life—no forks, no branches, no alternate selves living alternate mornings in alternate cafés. Just this. Just here. Just now.

The Fork app said CHOOSE ONE PATH. Demanded it with neon urgency, fractured my life into numbered options, made every moment a referendum on all the others. And now this little app I forgot I downloaded, this pocket stranger, says: STAY PRESENT. Consistency matters.
The thesis, inverted. Made gentle. Not a demand but an invitation. Not a branching but a root.

Julian picks up his guitar again. A single note. Then another. The melody returns, soft as breathing, and the café fills with it, and the cats settle, and the sea keeps its ancient rhythm against the shore.
I close the meditation notification. I stay.
